source_text:
16. Identifying Second and Later Pages

a. General

(1) Identify second and later pages as a continuation page from the first page. Use a header that identifies the subject of the correspondence and the page number using the format shown below. Place the header at the left margin, 1 inch from the top of the page.

b. Examples of Continuation Page Header

(1) The subject line will be abbreviated if too long for one line. Abbreviate only those words that lend themselves to common abbreviations.

EXAMPLE: (Single line abbreviation)
Subj: SECNAV FOREIGN AREA OFFICER PROGRAM

(2) When the subject is too long for one line, use block style paragraph format.

EXAMPLE: (2-line abbreviation)
Subj: DEFENSE POLICY ANALYSIS - TASK
FORCE REVIEW
plain_language_rule: Identify second and later pages with a continuation page header placed at the left margin, 1 inch from the top of the page. The header includes the abbreviated subject and the page number. Abbreviate the subject line if too long for one line, using only words that lend themselves to common abbreviations. When the abbreviated subject is still too long for one line, use block style paragraph format.
renderer_impact: Place continuation header at left margin, 1 inch from top, on second and later pages; include abbreviated subject and page number; abbreviate long subjects using common abbreviations or use block style paragraph format for multi-line cases
notes: The header format shown includes only the subject and page number — no sender, addressee, or date elements are mentioned. The exact page-numbering format is not specified here; see C7-018. The manual states "1 inch from the top of the page" with no additional margin-bottom or line-spacing specifications. The phrase "words that lend themselves to common abbreviations" is subjective and leaves room for judgment. Block style paragraph format is the fallback when single-line abbreviation is insufficient.
